item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed this one and it's more than worthy of a +1 but there are still a few Americanisms that don't quite fit into an English fair such as the mention of Skee Ball (which I wouldn't even have heard of if not for the film Dogma) and chocolate sandwich cookies. Apart from those small issues an excellent Fair Folk Fair!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Obviously any faerie related phenomenon can be connected to SCP-2952.

Maybe:

Each access point is located no more than 1.5 km from the closest SCP-2952 terminal.

Inserted into the first paragraph where it is discussing the access points.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I dig this proposal, much more fluid than mine. Unless there is serious objection, when the timer ticks down Ihp's proposal will be used for the crosslink.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Because I had never read it before?

ETA: Noting that I added a crosslink to SCP-097 and reworked the SCP-1323 crosslink to he more like ihp's suggestion here for SCP-2523.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I like the link, but I feel it's a tad clumsily inserted and vague. Maybe instead:

Due to the overlapping time frames that SCP-1323 and SCP-2523 can be accessed, as well as the demeanors of the respective proprietors, a connection has been hypothesized. Inquiries regarding SCP-2523 have resulted in the aforementioned large entities appearing and demanding a cessation of this line of questioning. Failure to comply has resulted in permanent ejection, similar to threatening fairground staff.

While we're at it, I'm wondering why SCP-097 wasn't linked to 2523.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as shown below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

SCP-1323 and SCP-2523 are both similar to each other, as each involves spatial distortion, and strange entities making odd exchanges. This similarity has not gone unnoticed, too, as 2523 actually links back to 1323 on that point. It only seems fitting that 1323 might link there as well. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Adding the following paragraph at the end:

The similarities between the content within SCP-1323 and SCP-2523 anomalous regions of space has been noted, and it is likely both may be highly correlated phenomena. Investigation into a shared origin or history between the two anomalies is ongoing.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So this one popped over the +100 mark just in time for the State Fair that inspired it to roll around again. Coincidence? Probably. :-P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if someone (Let's say a D class) buys a crap ton of tickets and then get's terminated, wouldn't that be an easy way for the foundation to get a lot of tickets without worrying about in-debt D-classes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can see your confusion. Basically, the person who actually buys the tickets is the one who owes the price, regardless if they give the tickets to someone else, and that debt is created at the moment of purchase, not the moment of redemption of the tickets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My understanding of a fair is you buy $10 worth of tickets from the booth, you go on a ride and give those tickets to the owner of the ride, at the end of the day the owner of that ride hands in your tickets back to the booth and get's his $10 (Minus whatever the cut is) So while the tickets are worthless to normal people, they have value to ride owners.

…the value to whichever one of the Fair Folk currently is in possession of it

This makes me think that the Fun fair works the same way as above and the Fair folk are individual people rather then just one big collective (English Vocabulary Lacking)

So if the fun fair of working on that system and if i'm reading correctly, the actual favuor/emotion/ect can't be taken until a person has paid for a ride with his/her ticket and given their ticket to the fair folk that owns the ride, who then uses it for it's own purposes.

But then it kind of contradicts with the following line

Civilians and Foundation personnel who purchase tickets will display a variety of mental, emotional, and behavioral abnormalities for up to 7 years.

This kind of says that the value of the ticket is taken regardless of if the user has spent their tickets or not. What if they don't spend their ticket after 7 years? What if they buy a ticket and then give it to someone else?

Also building up on the idea that each person is an individual, if a favour buys 100 tickets and a person spends those tickets on different rides, how do multiple people share a single favour?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are two parts to the problem. First, the fact is that the mentality of the Fair Folk is fundamentally alien to that of normal humanity. When a customer "buys" a ticket, the Fair Folk almost certainly interpret it as being given a promise or vow, which are fair more valuable that some trifling piece of paper. And that promise/vow/favor/debt is always up for interpretation so as to maximize the value to whichever one of the Fair Folk currently is in possession of it. So that "unusual compulsions up to 7 years later" is the Fair Folk calling in those promises for their own purposes.

I already gave an example of how the laugh/tear could be interpreted in different ways, so let's take a look at "a favor". Keep in mind that the favor will be collected in a way that benefits the Fair Folk, but not necessarily in a way that makes sense to the person owing the favor. So it could be something like smothering a spouse in their sleep or drowning a child, or it could be planting some marigolds in the front yard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh you are? In that case.
*cough* Can you be more clear/expand on how the tickets function please *cough*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wish the writer of this SCP would be more clear about the tickets

*coughiamtheauthorcough*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the Fair Folk do not think the same way you or I do.

I don't know about you, but I think reading this much SCP articles has my mind similar to someone in a mental asylums.

No one thinks like me. *Eye twitch*

never laugh or cry at that particular circumstance again

That makes MUCH more sense, I wish the writer of this SCP would be more clear about the tickets (Maybe experiment log?)
Thinking about it more, crying kind of has different "variations" of emotions. Do you get to choose which sad memory you give up? Pretty sure that dropped ice cream when you were 5 might not be the same worth as that dead parent The Fair folk do not think the same way as us. The Fair folk do not think the same way as us. Stop over thinking everything.
Psychotic breakdown

My one ticket:
Sorrowful Tear: Reading The Harbinger
Joyful Laugh: Reading the discussion thread in The Harbinger

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also 10 joyful laughs and 10 sorrowful tears is equal to a year and a day? Must have been a pretty emotionless year.

Ah, but remember that the Fair Folk tend to interpret things in a rather… abstract way. Yes, it could literally mean that you owe them a single laugh and a single tear at one time and place, but what if they interpret it to mean that you can never laugh or cry at that particular circumstance again. You laughed at your spouse's joke and now you'll never laugh at them again, no matter what. You cried at the death of your parent and now you'll never cry in circumstances involving family, ever again.

I'm not saying that that's definitely what will happen, but just remember that the Fair Folk do not think the same way you or I do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Entrants in any category receive "free passes" to SCP-1323 and are allowed access to "employee restricted" areas."… "Fairground employees can occasionally be seen moving through these passages, although they will uniformly escort non-employees back to the original chamber if seen."

Should this be non-employees and Entrants seeing that they have "free passes" into these areas?

Also I am sitting on the fence for this one, nice idea but i think it can have a little more information about the ticket price.

Also 10 joyful laughs and 10 sorrowful tears is equal to a year and a day? Must have been a pretty emotionless year.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: very nice, but i kinda feel like there wasnt enough to the ticket costs, like there shouldve been a log for things when d-class bought tickets.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One thing that seems missing is a description of the attendee's reactions to the anomalous things going down at the fair. From Unicorns to the cost of tickets, these would seem to get a reaction from the average Joe, and if there were no raised eyebrows than the taking it all in stride should be noticed as an anomalous effect. Aside from this minor note, +1 all around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a random aside, but this song reminded me of this article

Vernian Process- "Something Wicked (That Way Went)"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, this is why I come to this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Equines( various breeds of horses and unicorns)"

And then I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yyyeah, a couple of people said that in chat. Definitely wasn't an influence, though, as I haven't played WoW in quite a while and not since they put Darkmoon into its own zone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not just you, as soon as I saw the access restrictions I was worried it was going to be an bad WoW import. Happily it was neither. =]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[…] can only be accessed from one of four access points […]

Each access point is active for 7-12 consecutive days […]

Only 37% of employees appear to be human, while the remainder are anomalous humanoids.

… am I the only person who thought of the Darkmoon Faire?

I do like it, I just found myself giggling at that thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how this SCP isn't explicitly scary or horrifying (like some Wondertainment stuff), but it only very softly implicates what could be going on. The ticket prices tab is one of those parts. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, I haven't seen it. I'd heard of it, but never had any of the premium cable channels.

Also, I imagine some researcher somewhere desperately trying to avoid calling them unicorns: "Pure white, shaggy-coated equine with cloven hooves, bearing a single horn with clockwise spiral placed in the center of the forehead. Extremely aggressive and proficient with using its horn to attack any human being that has ever engaged in sexual activity." "…fuck it, it's a unicorn."

item.title: Nice
item.content:encoded: Kind of surprised a fey faire hasn't been written up yet! Nice take on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All attempts to interview employees, or interact with them in any way other than as part of their duties, are rebuffed with suggestions to "take it up with management." No member of "management" has ever been located despite repeated requests for interviews and exploration of restricted areas.

Heh, have you ever seen the show Carnivale?

I love this— particularly the consequences of disrupting the Fair. Hits just the right note, like the Fair is actively resisting the scientific tone of the article. That disjunction was probably my favorite thing about this— how do you describe the Fae like a plane crash or a thing in a petri dish?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat. Neat neat neat.

I like that merely being exposed to the anomaly, just going to the fair, isn't enough to make The Bads happen, you have to buy in somehow. I also dig the list of ticket prices, that creeped me out.

Mostly I just like it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the input. I'd actually meant to include cotton candy/candy floss and caramel/toffee apples, but forgot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It sounds like the fair from Stardust, especially the ticket prices being memories and shizzle.
I like it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a Brit and the 'fair food' bit is a bit too American to fit-I've never seen corn dogs or cookies at the fairs I've visited in London and Oxford. I'd replace those with cotton candy, toffee apples and donuts (or candy floss and doughnuts, to use the UK forms). Though I realise that the fair folk may do things differently…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
100 tickets: a favor

… and this is where it went from "awwwwww" to "eeeeeeeee". Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You'll have to wait until she gets back. The 'I ATE'NT DEAD' sign is up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice without going down the Renaissance Fair path. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Probably best to call them the Lords and Ladies, just to be safe.

/I'll go find Weatherwax.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would be a Fairly Bad Idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if we called them… Fairly Odd Parents?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the Sidhe of the old stories would tear you in half for calling them fairies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, they're not fairies. They're Fair Folk. A world of difference. Like the difference between housecats and Kzinti.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a really obvious mythological creature (fairies) adapted into an SCP, and it's a pun (It's a Fairy Fair, get it?) but it's good, and not bad.

This is the most confused I've ever been. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of the Vocaloid song dark woods circus. In a good way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A simple concept, executed well, with lots to like. I love that you just throw in unicorns and deep-fried ambrosia (which may be the best thing ever) ever so casually. Lots of great fairy tale hallmarks in this too. Easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The prize coins are bagged in aurochs leather, and aurochs have been extinct since 1627… combine that with the "sometimes people who eat the food can't leave", and I think this puts the "fair" in "fairy"… upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having just read the Night Circus again, this article reminds me of it, in a good way.+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think so, since non-Euclidian might be interpreted as something that isn’t anomalous at all, which would make the sentence sound weird, like Kalinin said.

Regardless, the concept on this one earns my upvote before I even begin analyzing the execution. Nice job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Topologically Inconsistent" is now my favorite name suggestion for a band.
Article related, I effing love this. Fantastic and old-timey (with anachronisms) fair? Sign me up! Payment in something more significant than something so simple as money? You betcha! No lolyoudie thrown about is a plus. Makes me want to read more in a more subjective view, or something that just tells _more_.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps "topologically inconsistent" would be better?

I think that would be an excellent way to put it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, I meant non-Euclidian more in the sense that there are passageways that should intersect each other, that don't. Like, you go 100', turn right & go 10', turn right & go 10', turn right and go 100'… but never actually intersect with the original passage. Perhaps "topologically inconsistent" would be better?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nicely done. There's lots of little details to chew on as one reads this, and I like the idea of an anomalous phenomenon that's more mischievious than outwardly hostile (though the mischief gets fairly hostile).

One thing:

a complex system of passageways whose layout does not conform to Euclidian geometry.

This is silly. "Non-Euclidean" is not the same as "physically impossible". Non-euclidean geometry, without getting too far into it, refers to elliptic and hyperbolic geometry, which are actually more applicable to the actual physical space we inhabit than traditional geometry. Just say "does not conform to current understanding of physics" or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the whole thing is supposed to be a little "off", so I guess I'll take that as a compliment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot, but I feel like there's something off about it that isn't supposed to be. Nevertheless, it's done well enough for me to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New SCP, partly inspired by the state fair that's going on right now in my area.
